text:
What does restocking look like? 

Here are my thoughts. 

#oott

quotedTweet.text:
Months of deinventory. Now restocking creates three fragmented paths.

▸ SPR slow, budget-driven, no rush next quarter.
▸ China fast, price-reactive.
▸ Commercial stockpiles flex on spreads.
▸ Storage upside if contango holds.
▸ Structural demand destruction: the real risk.

@JuneGoh_Sparta examines what's next. 

Read the full brief here: https://t.co/KHkUgxaWmr
#oilmarkets #crudeoil #commoditytrading #oott

text: Read here https://t.co/lIVw7rve9m

I do need to clarify that the 'overhang' is short term on the operational front.

There is still 1 billion barrels missing from the market and part of that has to be replenished somehow.

But from a trading window perspective where Asian refineries are buying crude for August loaders, they have enough. All this prompt oil coming out of the SoH is adding length to the physical trading market.

The underlying crude shortfall is still there - just a question on how to replenish. I'll issue another note about this point soon.

#oott

quotedTweet.text:
Quoted in Bloomberg today:

“Asian refineries are already well supplied till August, and the prompt barrels released from the Strait of Hormuz simply push the balances to an overhang, without China picking up on demand,” said June Goh a senior oil market analyst at Sparta Commodities.

https://t.co/iKDQEbqeb5

Oil Prices Retreat to Pre-War Level as Supply Concerns Ease

Crude oil prices and calendar spreads have largely reverted to pre-war levels as traders anticipate a rapid normalisation of exports through the Strait of Hormuz and the smooth replenishment of depleted oil inventories around the world.

Front-month Brent crude futures retreated to $77 per barrel on June 23, down from a wartime high of $126 and the lowest since the United States and Israel launched a surprise attack on Iran at the end of February.

Prices have retreated fastest for contracts nearest to expiry causing the backwardation to soften and the futures curve to flatten ...

Heavy downside pressure is slamming the base metals complex. On the macro side, it's getting choked out by the USD rally, but the real pain is coming from CTAs aggressively puking shorts across the board.

Yesterday (June 23rd), CTAs stepped on the gas, accelerating systematic selling across the major venues. Kpler data shows CTAs flushed roughly 11,475 lots out of Comex copper and clipped around 2,834 lots from LME copper.

They didn't stop there—they dumped about 1,352 lots of LME aluminum, flipping net short for the first time since September 2025, and they’re continuing to hit the bid in both tin and nickel.

#copper #aluminum

#Gold's break below USD 4,000 points to continued long liquidation from investors as the metal extends its 26% correction from January's record high. The technical breakdown has further undermined sentiment, forcing additional position reductions despite an underlying macro backdrop that has started to improve over the past week.

The main headwinds remain a stronger US dollar and continued ETF outflows, while speculative positioning has already been reduced significantly. At the same time, several of the factors that fuelled the recent correction are beginning to soften. The sharp collapse in crude oil prices is easing inflation concerns, reducing the need for further Fed tightening. That shift is increasingly reflected in Fed funds futures, where expectations for additional rate hikes have faded, while longer-dated Treasury yields have also moved lower.

Taken together, this suggests the fundamental backdrop is becoming less hostile for gold, even if investor sentiment remains fragile. For now, the market needs to see ETF selling stabilise and the dollar lose momentum before bargain hunters regain confidence. Until then, price action is likely to remain driven more by positioning and technicals than by fundamentals.
https://t.co/kUSHsYWyFL

Daily Digest-Night Update 6/24

📷DOW NASDAQ S&P 500 RUSSELL 2000

US stocks ended mixed as tech and semiconductor weakness persisted while falling oil and Fed stress test results supported cyclicals and financials ahead of key inflation and growth data

The Dow closed higher while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq extended their losing streak to a third session amid continued pressure on growth stocks tied to AI valuation concerns and earlier chip sector selling.

Tech and semiconductor names lagged, with Qualcomm (QCOM), Sandisk (SNDK) and the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) edging lower, while Cerebras (CBRS) fell after issuing a disappointing post-IPO margin outlook.
Energy stocks weakened as crude (@CL) prices slid toward prewar levels on increased Middle East supply, while lower oil costs supported airlines and travel names and eased inflation expectations.
Financials advanced after major banks including JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Goldman Sachs (GS) passed the Federal Reserve’s annual stress test, announced share repurchases and dividend hikes, and were deemed able to withstand large losses.
Sunrun (RUN) rose sharply after striking a deal with Tesla (TSLA) to supply power for AI data centers, making it one of the session’s strongest gainers.
Wendy’s (WEN) jumped following a Reddit-driven meme rally focused on its high short interest and the hiring of a new CFO, with heavy retail trading activity pushing the stock intraday to multi-month highs.
Homebuilders such as KB Home (KBH) gained after passage of an affordable housing bill, lifting the group on expectations of increased demand and supportive policy tailwinds.
After the close, Micron (MU) reported Q3 results that beat revenue and EPS expectations and issued strong AI-driven Q4 guidance, sending the shares significantly higher in after-hours trading following earlier intraday weakness.
Traders are looking ahead to Thursday’s PCE inflation report, a GDP revision and weekly jobless claims for further clarity on the Fed’s policy path, along with market follow-through from Micron (MU)’s earnings and other corporate results.
https://t.co/EH4FbiAMoN

It’s exactly the kind of message the Western political class would rather you never hear.

text:
Good Morning! 

Futures are up, with ES +27

Oil prices are lower this morning, with WTI crude down to $71 a barrel. 

The techs are looking better this morning. 

South Korea's KOSPI regained ground after yesterday's tech driven sell-off. 

The market is waiting on $MU earnings which will drop later today after the bell. This report will be a critical indicator of semiconductor demand and AI spending. The market can see a big move in the AI tech stocks, led by chips tomorrow. 

$NVDA is leading the Mag 7 this morning. 

$GOOGL will join the Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon $VZ, effective Monday, June 29, expanding mega cap tech presence in the blue chip average.

$TWLO is up 4% pre-market on renewed analyst optimism, including a Goldman Sachs Buy initiation with a $300 target, building on the their raised 2026 outlook and AI platform momentum following a successful turnaround.

The AI chip supply chain is facing a critical bottleneck at the manufacturing and testing layer, where optical interconnect scaling and silicon photonics capacity are emerging as key constraints on AI data center buildout. This is bullish for $ALAB $CRDO $AAOI $CIEN $COHR $FN $GLW $LITE. 

I don't see any other notable pre-market activity or significant news. 

I expect to see very little action in the market ahead of $MU earnings later today. 

The market may trade flat and range bound with very little volume for most of the day, with some movement at the end of the day as people do some last minute positioning ahead of those earnings.

US Stocks Set to Recover After Sharp Tech Selloff as Focus Shifts to Micron Earnings

U.S. stock futures are pointing higher Wednesday morning as markets look to stabilize following Tuesday’s sharp tech-led decline. The selloff was driven by weakness in semiconductor and big tech stocks, but investors are now turning their attention to $MU’s upcoming earnings.

Futures Snapshot:
- E-Mini S&P 500 up 0.25% at $7455.75
- E-Mini Nasdaq 100 up 0.58% at $29838.00
- E-Mini Dow down 0.03% at $52064.00

Oil Prices:
- WTI Crude down 1.86% near $71.85
- Brent Crude down 1.69% at $75.50

Key Market Context:
Markets are attempting a recovery after a difficult Tuesday session in which chip stocks and several Magnificent Seven names came under heavy pressure. The rebound in futures is being supported by bargain hunting ahead of $MU’s third-quarter results, which are due after the market close and are seen as a major test for the AI and memory chip trade.

Premarket Movers:
- $MU is up nearly 4% as investors position ahead of earnings. The stock had dropped more than 13% on Tuesday.
- Cerebras Systems is down more than 14% in premarket despite beating expectations in its first earnings report as a public company.
- FedEx is down around 7% after delivering mixed results and cautious full-year guidance.
- Twilio is up 5% after Goldman Sachs initiated coverage with a Buy rating and a $300 price target.
- Wendy’s is surging nearly 14% amid retail investor interest and short-squeeze speculation.

Asia Market Update:
South Korea’s Kospi Index is rebounding strongly after Monday’s roughly 10% plunge. The benchmark rose 3.26% on Wednesday as local investors bought the dip in technology stocks. The Kospi remains one of the best-performing major indices globally this year, up more than 100%.

Economic Calendar:
Today’s key data releases include:
- U.S. new home sales for May (10:00 a.m. ET)
- U.S. leading economic indicators for May (10:00 a.m. ET)
- Weekly crude oil inventories (10:30 a.m. ET)
- $70 billion 5-year Treasury note auction result (1:00 p.m. ET)

Overall Market View:
Sentiment is showing signs of stabilization after Tuesday’s tech rout, with investors shifting focus toward $MU’s earnings as a potential catalyst for the semiconductor and AI sectors. While the broader market remains cautious, the combination of oversold conditions in some tech names and expectations around $MU’s results is supporting a modest recovery in futures. Markets will be watching closely to see whether $MU can deliver strong enough results to restore confidence in the AI trade.

🐦 Z Pulse

• Oil Collapse: Brent crude falls to $75, lowest since February. Trump orders DOJ probe into oil companies for price manipulation while prices crater. Conflicting narrative on who's inflating what.

• Turkey MSCI Ultimatum: MSCI demands transparency reforms (ownership disclosure, market surveillance) by November 2026 or Turkey faces potential index removal. Heavy accusations per local analysts.

• $MU Earnings Watch: Micron reports today, market in wait-and-see mode. AI chip sector whipsawed after $NVDA AGM and margin concerns at peers like $CBRS. Space stocks ($ASTS, $RKLB) struggling while AI infrastructure attracts capital.

• AI Chip M&A: $QCOM acquires Modular Inc. for ~$4B to enable cross-platform AI software deployment. SK Hynix Nasdaq listing July 10, targeting $30B+ raise for memory chip capacity.

📰 News Flow

• Iran-Dollar Talks: Treasury Secretary Bessent says US-Iran negotiations include Tehran invoicing oil in dollars. Goldman cuts Q4 crude forecast to $80 citing US-Iran interim peace deal. IAEA dismisses "war of words," expects resumed inspections.

• Data Center Power Rush: $META and $MSFT lead $850B boom in data center leases. Multiple partnerships announced: $TSLA/$RUN team on AI data center energy, FuelCell/$FCEL partners with Fit Energy, Canadian nuclear firm AtkinsRealis seeks US approval for AI power tech.

• China Mineral Controls: Beijing detains two Japanese nationals over alleged rare earth smuggling, launches whistleblower hotline for export violations. Escalating minerals trade enforcement amid tech rivalry.

📊 Market Direction Read

• Bias: Cautious — Futures mixed, oil plunging, tech recovering from 3%+ $NDX selloff but awaiting $MU earnings as "gut check" for AI trade.

• Triggers:

• $MU earnings (afternoon) — memory pricing/AI demand signal for entire chip sector
• Oil volatility — Brent at $75, Trump DOJ probe vs. Iran deal conflicting narratives
• DXY above 101 pressuring gold, crypto ($BTC testing 59-60k support)

• Risk: $MU disappointment could reignite AI infrastructure skepticism after margin pressure at $CBRS; oil collapse may signal demand weakness beyond geopolitics.

🎯 On Watch Today

$MU earnings after close. Dan Ives (Wedbush) calls it a "gut check moment" for markets. Memory chip pricing and AI capex outlook will set tone for semiconductor complex heading into month-end.

Goldman Sachs cuts Q4 crude forecast to $80.

→ Gulf oil exports rebound to 60% of pre-war levels.
→ Markets question need for large security premium.
→ EV surge raises doubts on oil demand recovery.

Watch energy stocks, inflation trends, and currency moves.

🛢️ Goldman Sachs slashes Q4 crude oil forecast to $80/barrel, citing 3 key factors driving rapid price decline — analyst Daan Struyven, co-head of global commodities research, explains. #Gold #XAUUSD #Forex

ICYMI O/N

MACRO:

JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America and a collection of their peers all passed the Federal Reserve’s annual stress tests, a widely expected outcome after regulators last year began disclosing the exam’s scenarios and models in advance. Big banks responded with buybacks and dividend hikes. (WSJ)

'The Data-Center Boom Is Sparking a Third Wave of Inflation' WSJ article highlights how any deflation from the productivity gains from AI are being preceeded by the inflationary effects of the buildout, driving up prices of components, labour, and electricity. (WSJ)

Bitcoin is facing a massive options expiry that risks putting more pressure on the market. About $10 billion of notional value in Bitcoin options is set to expire on Deribit, the largest crypto options venue, at 4 p.m. Friday in Singapore. (BBG)

US President Trump engaged in a fiery clash over the Iran war with one of his top Republican critics, just hours after the president blindsided lawmakers by insisting they pass legislation to tighten voter-eligibility rules before he would sign a bill focused on lowering housing costs. (WSJ)

An LNG tanker owned by ADNOC began sending a signal from within the Persian Gulf, as more vessels broadcast their journeys and intentions in recent days following an interim peace deal between the US and Iran. (BBG)

The price of insurance for vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz has fallen by more than half over the past six days, reducing costs for individual ships by hundreds of thousands of dollars. (FT)

Iraq will be compelled to consider all available options if its OPEC quota is not significantly increased, a senior Iraqi oil ministry official told Reuters on Thursday. Separate sources told Reuters Iraqi officials had considered leaving OPEC, but the current plan was to remain a member and seek a higher quota. (RTRS)

South Korea’s main stock exchange has delayed the launch of weekly options tied to single stocks, in a sign of growing concern about frothiness in the world’s best-performing equity market this year. (FT)

JPMorgan has boosted its Kospi bull target to 15,000. JPM recommends “adding on any dips and staying with maximum exposure in Korea... This is driven by a combination of our constructive view on AI and resultant hardware earnings”. They note the earnings of the tech firms are now “large enough to be meaningful at a macro level,” creating a wealth boost for corporates, households and the government. (BBG)

BoJ's Tamura (hawk) said the Bank should raise interest rates steadily towards neutral and consider speeding up the pace of tightening if inflation risks intensify. He recommended that the BOJ consider raising the policy rate by 25bps every few months, faster than market expectations for one hike at six month intervals. (WSJ)

Japan's government will call for monetary policy that bolsters private demand, a draft of its long-term economic blueprint reviewed by Reuters showed, signalling a preference for keeping borrowing costs low and setting up potential policy tensions with the central bank. The unusually explicit language underscores the Takaichi administration's growing unease with further rate hikes. (RTRS)

Japan’s 20-year bond auction drew the weakest demand since the market upheaval just over a year ago as worries about inflation and fiscal policy came back to the fore. (BBG)

Bond strategists are warning that Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s $2.3 trillion investment plan risks putting fresh pressure on Japan’s government debt market. (BBG)

Japan’s regional banks are selling more senior bonds as the Bank of Japan’s latest interest-rate increase intensifies competition for deposits. (BBG)

Japan's carmakers could see a $5.8 billion profit windfall this year due to the weak yen. (BBG)

Some major Chinese banks are shutting down services that aid retail trading in precious metals, after a multiyear rally in gold and silver went into reverse in recent months. (BBG)

China's central bank said it will debut overnight lending next week, offering reverse-repos to banks and brokers as it deepens control over what have been volatile short-term interest rates. (RTRS)

China has made breakthroughs in photonic chips, which transmit and process data using photons, and could help the country leapfrog the US in AI development. (SCMP)

HSBC expects China’s AI compute demand to boom and reach around 5GW by 2028, a fivefold increase from 2025, driven by explosive demand for large language model training and inference. (BBG)

China kicked off marketing up to €5 billion ($5.7 billion) of sovereign bonds in what could be its largest-ever such deal in euros. (BBG)

China will raise the amount of refined fuel its state-owned refiners are allowed to export in July to 800k metric tons from 600k metric tons in June, according to Reuters sources. (RTRS)

Direct financing through bonds and equity in China is increasingly overtaking bank lending as an important source of new funding, marking a structural shift in the financial system. (Shanghai Securities News)

China’s top auditor flagged several of the nation’s largest state-backed financial institutions for tax evasion and improper lending as Beijing seeks to rein in financial risks. (BBG)

China urged domestic rating firms to curb the concentration of top-tier AAA ratings in the country’s bond market, taking its most concrete step yet to better assess credit risks after years of record defaults. (BBG)

Trump said he had heard Andy Burnham was “extremely liberal”, in the US president’s first comments on Britain’s prospective prime minister. Asked what he knew about the former mayor of Greater Manchester, Trump said: “I don’t know anything. I see that he was, I guess, the mayor of a town. (FT)

Two of Britain’s biggest trade unions are increasing pressure on Andy Burnham not to pick Ed Miliband as the UK’s next finance minister, arguing that his North Sea oil policy has damaged jobs in the sector. (FT)

The European Union’s US trade deal is set to go into effect after the bloc gave its final sign-off, banking that the pact will ensure some stability despite ongoing economic tensions. (BBG)

A new assessment by Institut Montaigne, seen by the FT, found that the EU has implemented about 30 per cent of Draghi’s landmark competitiveness report and estimates that the rate could reach 60 per cent by the end of next year. (FT)

Consumer confidence in Germany picked up slightly as peace negotiations between Iran and the U.S. support the stabilization of sentiment, albeit at subdued levels. (WSJ)

Swiss President Guy Parmelin will meet US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer later this month for talks as the two countries aim to finalize a broader trade deal amid renewed tariff pressure from Washington. (BBG)

Australia’s unemployment rate fell and the economy added more jobs than expected in May, keeping the door open for the central bank to raise interest rates further in its bid to tame inflation. (WSJ)

Australian household spending rebounded in May as travel returned to normal after a war-induced drop in April, suggesting consumer demand was holding up in the face of higher borrowing costs and fuel prices. (RTRS)

Australia’s center-left government has successfully pushed through parliament its overhaul of taxation of the property market, with the government wagering the changes will make housing more affordable for younger Australians. (BBG)

Power demand from data centers is poised to surge over the next quarter-century, adding to an almost doubling of electricity consumption across Australia’s main grid by 2050, according to the network operator’s latest road map. (BBG)

New Zealand has seen a surge in Chinese tourists since it began testing an easier pathway for visitors via Australia, with a 40% increase in Chinese visitor arrivals. (BBG)

Thailand expects exports to reach a record $366.8 billion this year as global investment in artificial intelligence fuels demand for electronics made in the country. (BBG)

Thailand’s stock market is having the best year among Southeast Asian peers, as investors discover an unlikely source of exposure to the global artificial-intelligence boom. Much of that gain has come from Delta Electronics (Thailand) Pcl. (BBG)

Two powerful earthquakes hit Venezuela on Wednesday, causing buildings to collapse in the capital Caracas, rattling other cities and leaving dozens dead and hundreds injured. (WSJ)

GEOPOLITICAL:

Japan’s planned revisions to its key security documents have gone beyond a mere policy adjustment and represents a dangerous step toward a broader remilitarization agenda, the People’s Daily, Chinese Communist Party’s flagship newspaper, said in a commentary. (BBG)

EQUITIES:

Micron surged in late trading (+18%) after its quarterly sales forecast crushed Wall Street estimates, signaling that an AI-fueled growth run remains strong. Micron also secured 16 strategic customer agreements, which average three years in length, reducing pricing volatility by capping both upside and downside. CEO said the memory situation will persist beyond calendar 2027, adding that availability of memory chips may improve gradually in 2028. (BBG)

Qualcomm said it expects to generate $15 billion in sales from its data center business by 2029 as it moves beyond its core smartphone chips, sending shares more than 12% higher in after-hours trading on Wednesday. Arm Holdings, which supplies underlying technology for many Qualcomm chips, also rose 5% after the forecast. (RTRS)

'SK Hynix ADR Seen Driving 30% Upside as Micron Gap Narrows' Bloomberg article notes money managers and analysts say the discount in SK Hynix's shares to Micron's is looking harder to justify ahead of SK Hynix's American listing where SK is cementing its lead in high-bandwidth memory. (BBG)

Kioxia Holdings, the Japanese NAND memory supplier, plans to offer US depositary shares in the spring of 2027 and a stock split to take advantage of runaway demand for exposure to AI-related semiconductor stocks. (BBG)

Meta has replaced about 50% of human review requests with large language models this year, according to FT sources. Meta aims to reduce that figure further by the end of the year, by more than 90% for certain types of content. Meta is also attempting to reduce operating costs by using AI to automate internal processes such as coding. (FT)

Lockheed Martin nabbed a $35.35 billion contract from the U.S. government to quadruple production of its high-end Thaad missile-defense systems.The award is one of the first major procurement contracts executed under the Defense Department’s effort to stockpile weapons in the next few years. (WSJ)

Jefferies Financial missed second-quarter profit estimates on Wednesday as weakness in asset management overshadowed gains from robust dealmaking and equity underwriting. (RTRS)

Amazon added $13 billion to its planned India investments, accelerating its buildout of artificial intelligence and cloud infrastructure in the world’s most populous country. (BBG)

Anduril Industries is in talks to acquire Nissan Motor's Oppama assembly plant near Tokyo as the maker of autonomous weapons looks to build military drones in Japan, according to Reuters sources. (RTRS)

Chinese chip suppliers led by Huawei Technologies and Cambricon Technologies, together with tech giants developing their own silicon, are set to capture nearly 80 per cent of the domestic AI server market this year, further squeezing global rivals such as Nvidia, according to TrendForce. (SCMP)

Chinese copper manufacturer Zhejiang Hailiang Co. is betting its American customers won’t balk at higher prices if the US follows though on placing tariffs on the refined metal. (BBG)

Volkswagen is selling a 51% stake in engine subsidiary Everllence to Bain Capital, seeking to streamline its investment portfolio. (WSJ)

Aumovio SE’s negotiations to secure memory chips for next year are proving difficult as artificial intelligence companies consume supplies of the key components, according to the German auto supplier’s chief financial officer. (BBG)

EasyJet’s board said it has rejected a fourth takeover bid from U.S. investment firm Castlelake, but said it would extend talks and share limited commercial information in order to obtain a higher offer. (WSJ)

BP said on Thursday it has signed a concession agreement with ADNOC and partners to develop the Bab Gas Cap project in the UAE, taking a 10% stake. (RTRS)

Sky, the Comcast-owned British pay TV group, has agreed on terms to buy ITV's broadcast and streaming unit, according to Reuters sources, with ITV acquiring "The Great British Bake Off" producer Love Productions as part of the deal. (RTRS)

Halfords on Thursday forecast fiscal 2027 profit towards the top end of analyst expectations, after its annual earnings beat market views as its turnaround strategy gained traction. (RTRS)

H&M’s earnings were dented by one-off restructuring costs as it continues work to grow revenue through a series of product and customer experience strategies. (WSJ)

Daily Market Report #776

It is Thursday. Almost the end of the week, and I am still vibe coding, still locked in, and yesterday I spent the evening networking at a Sui event that changed how I see the next few years.

The room was full of lawyers, bankers, asset managers, all kinds of suits, and they are not here to dabble. They want to build on blockchain at full scale. They have had it with the old system, and they are coming to be the next Goldman, the next JPMorgan, bigger than BlackRock, on-chain.

Most of them do not like our version of crypto. The privacy, the cypherpunk ethos, the permissionless mess we love. They want the rails without the politics. They are ready to take over banking with this technology and leave the ideology behind.

The crypto market cap has now wiped out $2.3T in 8 months, more than half its value, falling from $4.3T in October to $2.0T.

Half the market, gone, in 8 months.

Fear & Greed at 12. $319M of the $401M in BTC liquidations overnight were longs, the longs getting flushed hard into the tech rout.

The US Dollar Index hit a 13-month high, which pulls liquidity from everything. US factory job cuts are near 2009 highs. Oil fell back below $80 and is now at pre-Iran-war levels as Gulf supply resumes, and the US Senate even voted to end the Iran war.

Bitcoin $61,255, down 2.3%. Dominance 56%. The $10.6B options expiry hits today, most positions out of the money, which adds whip to an already ugly tape. Hayes now says BTC likely bottoms around $40K. CryptoQuant says Strategy should pause its BTC buys.

Ethereum $1,632, down 2.2%. Vitalik confirmed the EF will cut its 2026 budget by roughly 40% on top of the 20% staff cut, and a 7-year ETH whale dumped 4,654 ETH into the weakness. The Foundation is shrinking hard exactly as the chain heads into its first-ever 3rd red quarter close, 6 days out.

But here is the number that complicates the doom: Ethereum has 13.2M monthly users, up 86% year over year, L1 fees down 48%, and $203B in tokenized assets sitting on it. The institution is in crisis and the network usage is at an all-time high at the same time.

Solana $68.24, down 1.8%, holding up better than ETH in the rout. The institutional build does not pause. That Sui-event energy is the same energy putting MoneyGram on as a validator and ICE and OKX into a tokenized securities JV this week.

XRP $1.08, down 2.2%. Lost $1.10. But the real-world wins keep landing: Ripple and SBI officially launched RLUSD in Japan after JFSA approval, and Ripple won preliminary EU MiCA approval. While the token bleeds, Ripple keeps clearing regulators on 2 continents. CLARITY text releases over July 4 for a final review per Lummis, with a House hearing now set for July 17, so the timeline is slipping past the July 4 signing hope toward mid-July.

Hyperliquid $63.60, up 3.6%. The one green major in my universe today, bouncing against the entire bleeding tape. After being the worst performer in the flush yesterday, it is the strongest today, which is exactly the high-beta whippiness that defines it. The venue dominance never broke.

The privacy thread tied straight into my Sui-event takeaway. StarkWare unveiled “Private KYC,” which is the most interesting needle-threading I have seen in a while: privacy that satisfies regulators. That is precisely the bridge between what the suits want and what the cypherpunks built, and it might be the thing that actually lets both worlds coexist. Quantstamp also formally confirmed the four-year-old Zcash Orchard counterfeiting bug, with no exploitation found, which closes that chapter cleanly. Zcash $413. Monero $313, down 2.5%, still the sector anchor.

Bittensor $220, the decentralized AI anchor holding firm. And the AI story of the day feeds the thesis perfectly: Anthropic accused Alibaba of illicitly extracting Claude’s capabilities in what it calls the largest known distillation attack to date. Then Alibaba sued the Pentagon over its blacklist. The AI cold war between the US and China is now playing out in lawsuits and alleged model theft, which is the exact nation-state-capture risk that makes uncensorable decentralized AI matter. When the frontier models are being stolen, walled, and litigated across borders, the case for permissionless compute writes itself.

Chainlink joined 47 banks for T+0 settlement. After a month of me having nothing new to say about it, that is an actual catalyst. 47 banks using the infrastructure for instant settlement. The integration finally has teeth. LINK $7.45, still bleeding.

Walking out of that event, I felt pumped. So many real institutions are building now. Maybe the days of getting rich fast off this are over, but our technology is taking over, and I would rather be early to that than early to another memecoin.

The price of Brent crude has fallen below the level seen before the war with Iran began, as a wave of accumulated oil reserves has begun to flow out of the Persian Gulf, according to the Financial Times.

Oil prices have plunged below $72.48/bbl—wiping out all gains since the February 28 Iran war.
As shipping traffic gradually restarts through the critical Strait of Hormuz, Brent crude briefly dipped under pre-conflict levels before edging back to $72.63.

Oil prices extended their decline, approaching pre-war levels as tanker flows through the Strait of Hormuz resumed following a preliminary deal to ease regional tensions, reducing supply risks. Brent crude fell 0.5% to $73.34 per barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate slipped 0.4% to $70.07. The market has been weighed down by the faster-than-expected return of Middle East supply, even as efforts continue to fully restore shipping and clear remaining obstacles in the strait over the coming weeks.

#Oil prices fell for a fourth consecutive session, with #Brent slipping to $72.65/bbl and #WTI to $69.50/bbl, their lowest levels since before the Iran conflict. Improved tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and easing supply concerns have erased most of the geopolitical risk premium. Despite a sharp draw in U.S. crude inventories, rising fuel stockpiles added to the bearish sentiment.

OIL CRASHES TO PRE-WAR LEVELS – WTI BREACHES $70

Oil erased every single dollar of its Iran-war gains. Brent slipped below $73 for the first time since February 27, down 42% from its April 30 peak of $126.

The numbers:

• WTI: $69.32 (-1.45%)

• Brent: $72.52 (-1.65%)

• WTI within $3 of its pre-war price of $67.02

The catalyst: 
Tankers are flooding out of the Strait of Hormuz. 20M+ barrels exited in 24 hours. U.S. Energy Secretary Wright says flows are close to pre-war levels.

The signal: 
Brent's prompt spread flipped to bearish contango for the first time since the war started – immediate supply is abundant.

The catch: 
U.S. crude inventories hit their lowest since 1984 last week. Cushing stocks fell below operational levels. JPMorgan warns the market may be getting ahead of itself.

*WTI ANALYSIS:* WTI crude oil remained under heavy pressure as improving tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and progress in U.S.-Iran negotiations eased fears of supply disruptions. Expectations of increased Iranian exports and a faster normalization of global oil flows reduced the geopolitical risk premium that had previously supported prices. Although U.S. crude inventories continued to decline, the market largely focused on improving supply conditions, keeping oil near multi-month lows.

*WTI TECHNICAL ANALYSIS*

*WTI Technical Structure:* Strong Bearish Candle on Daily, Trading Below 20-SMA on Daily, Gravestone Doji on 4H, Doji on 1H

*Weekly Trend:* Bearish

*Intraday Trend/ Intraday Strategy :* Bearish / Sell on Resistance, Sell on Support Breakdown

*Major Support:* 69.60, 67.50, 65.60

*Major Resistance:* 73.10, 75.00, 76.20

#Commodities

Brent #crudeoil trades below USD 73 and WTI below USD 70, extending a four-session decline that has all but erased the war-related risk premium. The reopening of the Strait of Hormuz has created a short-term wave of supply, pushing prices back towards levels that may prove difficult to sustain once the backlog of stranded barrels has cleared and releases from strategic reserves are scaled back. Longer term, however, slower demand growth driven by electrification and fuel switching may continue to cap upside potential. Meanwhile, over the past 11 weeks, combined US commercial and strategic crude inventories have fallen by 135 million barrels to a multi-decade low.

#Gold slumped below USD 4,000 to a seven-month low, pressured by a resurgent dollar and concerns that higher funding costs may yet lie ahead. The technical breakdown and a 29% correction from January's record high continue to weigh on investor sentiment, leaving the near-term outlook challenged despite steady central bank demand providing an important source of underlying support. At the same time, a continued decline in energy prices and lower bond yields may eventually ease pressure on the Federal Reserve to raise rates.

#Silver, meanwhile, tumbled to a low of USD 55.6 and retracing 61.8% of the rally from its 2022 low at USD 17.56. With industrial metals also suffering a setback, silver is once again exposed to both precious and industrial metal weakness, helping drive the XAUXAG ratio higher towards 70.

#Copper broke key support at USD 6.15 on Wednesday, triggering a wave of technical selling and hedge fund long liquidation. As with precious metals, the main catalysts behind the slide to a seven-week low were a stronger dollar and the Fed's recent hawkish stance, both of which have weighed on the outlook for demand. Prices have since staged a modest rebound, but the technical outlook remains fragile below the mentioned level.

🛢️ WTI Crude Oil Market Update

• WTI crude oil remained under pressure as improving tanker movement through the Strait of Hormuz reduced concerns over global supply disruptions.
• Progress in U.S.-Iran negotiations increased expectations of higher Iranian oil exports, weighing on crude prices.
• The decline in geopolitical risk premium continued to support the bearish outlook.
• Despite falling U.S. crude inventories, traders remained focused on improving supply conditions.

• Technically, WTI maintains a bearish structure, trading below key moving averages with multiple reversal candlestick signals across lower timeframes.
• Key support and resistance levels remain critical for short-term trading opportunities.

Welcome to Mid-Week Trade! Wednesday was a mixed day on Wall Street, headlined by big bearish moves in the metals markets. Both gold and silver put in major sell-offs amid a suddenly stronger USD. Key natural support levels bit the dust as metals bidders stayed on the sidelines.

On the economic news front, Wednesday’s key item was the weekly EIA crude oil inventories report. Inventories fell by more than 6 million barrels for the week and now stand 7% below the seasonally-adjusted five-year average. In addition, the United States Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) is currently at its lowest level since 1983. In short, US oil supplies are down dramatically as a result of the war in Iran.

It’s been a brutal June for #gold. The market has finally broken out of a 60-day consolidation period and is on the run south beneath $4000. Here’s a support zone worth watching for the coming weeks in the #XAUUSD:

Monthly 62% Retracement, 3706.28
Monthly Bollinger Mid-Point, 3703.34

Check out our Key Levels for more information on this Trade Idea.

At press time, the MoU between Iran and the US is holding firm. We’ll have the latest on the situation tomorrow at 1:00 PM EST over at Switch Markets on YouTube. See you there!

text: $USO burning through oil right now EIA Crude Oil Inventories Actual -6.088M

U.S. SESSION: HOUSING CRACKS, OIL TANKS DESPITE SUPPLY SQUEEZE

May new home sales plunged 7.3% to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 580,000 units – well below the 638,000 forecast and down from April's upwardly revised 626,000.

The median new home price held at $424,900, but the average price rose 5.0% year-over-year. Prices aren't falling – buyers just can't afford them.

🛢️ EIA CRUDE INVENTORIES: BEAT THE ESTIMATE, BUT OIL STILL CRASHED

Headline: 
Crude inventories fell -6.1M barrels (forecast -3.9M, previous -8.3M). Total commercial stocks dropped to 412.1M barrels – about 7% below the 5-year average. 

Strategic reserves fell to 331.2M barrels – the lowest since 1983. Cushing inventories hit their lowest since October 2014.

Yet WTI crashed over 4.67% to ~$70, breaking below the $70 level.

The disconnect: 
Refineries are processing oil at record rates, and exports remain elevated. But gasoline inventories rose +2.064M barrels (forecast -0.578M) and distillates rose +3.064M barrels (forecast -0.505M). 

Finished products are piling up faster than consumers can use them – suggesting weaker domestic demand.

The bigger driver: 
the U.S.-Iran peace deal has unwound the wartime risk premium. The IMO has confirmed safety guarantees for tankers crossing the Strait of Hormuz. WTI is now trading roughly 40% below its conflict peak.

$WTI $USO $XHB $SPY $TLT

quotedTweet.text:
It’s a breakdown of the key Asian session economic data releases from the table (Japan & Australia):

🇯🇵 Japan – SPPI y/y (Services PPI)

· Actual: 3.3% (in line with forecasts)

· Previous: 3.4% (revised down slightly)

· Takeaway: No surprise here. The slight dip from prior isn't large enough to shift BoJ policy expectations, so JPY reaction is muted.

🇦🇺 Australia – Mixed but mostly weaker data

1. Retail Sales m/m (implied by the -0.7% figure)
   · Actual: -0.7% (sharp miss vs -0.4% forecast)
   · Previous: +0.4%
   · Takeaway: A significant contraction in consumer spending. This is bearish for AUD.

2. Monthly CPI y/y (table labels it "CPI m/m")
 
· Actual: 4.0% (below 4.3% forecast & below 4.2% previous)

· Takeaway: Headline inflation cooled more than expected.

3. Quarterly CPI y/y
· Actual: 4.0% (below 4.3% forecast & down from 4.4% previous)

· Takeaway: Confirms disinflationary trends, reducing RBA rate-hike pressure.

4. Trimmed Mean CPI q/q (core inflation)
· Actual: 0.4% (beat 0.3% forecast; previous 0.3%)

· Takeaway: Core inflation ticked up slightly, offering a tiny offset to the weak headline.

Overall Session Sentiment:

· AUD is under pressure – weak Retail Sales combined with softer headline CPI outweigh the slight core beat, reinforcing dovish RBA expectations.

· JPY is neutral – the SPPI print changed nothing.

In FX, expect AUDJPY to face downside risks following this mix.

Iraq rattling the cage matters more than people think. If OPEC discipline cracks, supply rises and oil softens... that's the import-bill relief emerging markets like Pakistan have been praying for. One member's frustration becomes everyone's else's problem...

quotedTweet.text:
🇮🇶🛢️ Iraq to consider all options if OPEC quota not raised significantly and has weighed an exit.

Iraq is OPEC's 2nd largest producer & founding member (OPEC was founded in Baghdad).

Their economy, along with many others, is under strain from Hormuz disruptions amid the Iran war. Baghdad wants the quota raised in line with production capacity & population.

"Iraq will be compelled to consider all available options" if it is not taken seriously.

No matter how you look at it, this war has at least shifted the mindset for countries who rely heavily on goods transported in and out of the Strait of Hormuz. 

It’s time to explore alternative options… or risk going through this likely multiple times. 

Source: Reuters: / Writer: Jamie

text: 🔴 Iraq Oil Ministry denies reports of OPEC exit, says they don't reflect official position.
Just in | Iraqi Oil Ministry Reports Strong OPEC Support for Iraq's Unique Circumstances.
IBM unveils 0.7nm chip: 70% performance boost, but not yet mass-produced. Iraq denies leaving OPEC, calming oil markets. Caracas stock exchange halts trading after earthquake.

Update: #Iraq’s Oil Ministry has now issued a clarification, saying reports suggesting Baghdad threatened to end its @OPECSecretariat membership “do not reflect the official position” of the government.

The ministry stresses that Iraq remains committed to addressing production ceilings through #OPEC+‘s established technical mechanisms, including the ongoing review of members’ maximum sustainable production capacity by an independent consultant.

In many ways, this reinforces the broader point: Iraq’s objective appears less about leaving OPEC than about strengthening its position within it and securing production levels that better reflect its long-term capacity (check quoted tweet). #oott

quotedTweet.text:
NEW🇮🇶: @_HadleyGamble’s interview with #Iraq’s new prime minister Ali al-Zaidi (@IraqiPMO) contains three major energy headlines. A 🧵

1/
• A warning that Iraq could “suspend” its @OPECSecretariat membership if prevented from producing in line with its “capabilities”.
• US energy companies to receive priority for investment.
• 500,000 b/d of Iraqi crude earmarked to replenish the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPRs).

Viewed together, they’re really one story, where the new PM isn’t just announcing policy, but “attempting” to redefine his country’s strategic leverage at time of shifting tectonic plates in the region.

2/
The #OPEC+ comments are the most eye-catching, but they’re also the LEAST surprising.

For years, successive Iraqi leaders have argued that the country’s production quota fails to reflect its reserves, production potential, population and reconstruction needs — those are their words.

This didn’t begin with the new prime minister, nor with former PM @mohamedshia. It’s becoming institutional Iraqi policy — (the Baghdad ruling elite’s NEED, I would say).

3/
I’ve been hearing versions of this argument since before al-Sudani entered office.

But Al-Sudani called for quota revisions as a candidate for the premiership, a prime minister-designate, & repeated them publicly while in office, and Iraq made the case directly before #Opec+ officials during his tenure.

More recently, SOMO director-general Ali Nazar al-Shatari echoed many of the same in a string of interviews.

4/
That consistency matters because Baghdad’s frustration has never been solely about “barrels”.

IMHO, it’s about influence.

5/
From conversations over recent years, Iraqi officials have repeatedly pointed to the #UAE’s successful baseline revision as evidence that production allocations can evolve, and many quietly believed Iraq deserved similar consideration.

Following the UAE’s exit from #OPEC/OPEC+, Baghdad increasingly appears focused on strengthening its weight within the group, not simply securing a higher quota.

6/
Ironically, Iraq may be making its strongest case at a time when quota politics could become less restrictive.

As non-OPEC supply growth from the US, Guyana, Brazil and Mexico gradually moderates while demand expands next year, the market may naturally create more room for additional OPEC barrels.

That makes today’s messaging less about immediate production and more about shaping tomorrow’s negotiations. But Baghdad still has to prove its point during the MSC assessment, which @OPECSecretariat needs to provide an update on BTW.

7/
The Washington message is equally significant.
Prioritizing US investment while earmarking crude for SPR replenishment would deepen Iraq’s strategic energy relationship with Washington.
That fits a broader pattern.

Baghdad has spent recent years attracting Chinese investment through licensing rounds while simultaneously advancing major projects with Western firms including BP and TotalEnergies. It has been in talks with @Chevron and @exxonmobil as well. 

Energy is becoming Iraq’s preferred language of strategic balance.

8/
Of course, announcements are easier than implementation.

Instability, repeated tension with Erbil and export infrastructure remains Iraq’s biggest constraint. 
Southern terminals still dominate exports, while alternative routes through Turkey, Jordan, Syria and Oman remain at best, works in progress. 

Likewise, promises to tackle corruption and disarm militias will ultimately be judged by delivery not headlines.

9/
The real takeaway from this interview isn’t that Iraq is preparing to leave OPEC.
It’s that Iraq is becoming more assertive in defining the terms of its participation.

How will that be received in the Kingdom🇸🇦, that remains to be seen👀.

(… to be continued) #oott
